elements.sent-2411-001: Ergodicity has been one of the fundamental concepts underpinning our understanding of thermalisation in isolated systems since the first developments in classical statistical mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, a similar notion has been introduced for quantum systems, termed Complete Hilbert Space Ergodicity (CHSE), in which the evolving quantum state explores all of the available Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-003: This contrasts with the Eigenstate Thermalisation Hypothesis (ETH), in which thermalisation is formulated via the properties of matrix elements of local operators in the energy eigenbasis.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work we explore how ETH-violation mechanisms, including quantum many-body scars and Hilbert space fragmentation can affect Complete Hilbert Space Ergodicity.
elements.sent-2411-005: We find that the presence of these mechanisms leads to CHSE in decoupled subspaces, a phenomenon we call Hilbert Subspace Ergodicity, and which represents a protocol for constructing $t$-designs in subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-006: One of the key developments in statistical physics has been the introduction of ergodicity.
elements.sent-2411-007: A classical system is deemed to be ergodic if all of the available points in phase space are explored with equal probability over long timescales \cite{Bol-1896 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Such a notion however, appears to be inconsistent with dynamics of closed quantum systems, due to the constraints imposed by their unitary evolution.
elements.sent-2411-009: This has spurred significant research interest into how thermalisation can be understood in isolated quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: The most widely accepted framework in this area is the eigenstate thermalisation hypothesis (ETH) \cite{Deu-91a, Sre-94a, Rig-08a, Deu-18a .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The ETH states that the apparent thermalisation of quantum systems occurs due to the fact that eigenstates of large, generic many body systems appear indistinguishable from thermal states at the level of local observables.
elements.sent-2411-012: The formulation of ETH also led to the exploration of different forms of ETH-violation, which can be subdivided into strong and weak ETH-violation.
elements.sent-2411-013: The former include systems which exhibit integrability \cite{Cal-16 , many-body localisation (MBL) \cite{Nan-15a, Aba-19 , or strong Hilbert space fragmentation (HSF) \cite{Sal-20, Mou-22a, Mou-22c, Han-24 , while the latter refer to systems with quantum many-body scars (QMBS) or weak HSF \cite{Tur-18a, Pap-22a, Cha-23a, Ser-21a, Des-22a, Doo-21a, Doo-23a .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Both phenomena have also been investigated in digital quantum circuits \cite{Han-24, Log-24a, And-24 , as these have been rising to prominence both due to their usage to probe interesting physical phenomena \cite{Vov-21, Sta-22, Sum-23, Kee-23 , as well as fascinating systems on their own \cite{Ber-18, Ber-19, Sum-24 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In contrast to ETH, recently Pilatewski et al. \cite{Pil-23a introduced the concept of Complete Hilbert Space Ergodicity (CHSE).}
elements.sent-2411-016: This notion of ergodicity is defined at a dynamical level, and refers to the phenomenon by which any initial state will explore all other states within the Hilbert space with ``equal probability'' in the long time limit.
elements.sent-2411-017: This opens up several research questions.
elements.sent-2411-018: All examples of CHSE so far are for dynamics with global unitaries: does it persist when the dynamics have a local character, e.g., in local circuit models? What is the effect of ETH-violating mechanisms on the dynamics of the systems which would otherwise exhibit CHSE? What is the effect of symmetries?.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{schematic_comparison.png} \caption{Schematic representation for Complete Hilbert Space Ergodicity (A) vs. Complete Hilbert Space Ergodicity in a Sub- space (B). In the former, the system explores all of the available $D$-dimensional Hilbert space, represented by a 3-dimensional box. In the latter however, the system can only explore a $B$-dimensional subspace of the full Hilbert space, represented by a 2-dimensional horizontal slice. In the long time limit, the system represented in (A) will explore all regions within the box with equal probability, whereas the system in (B) will explore just the shaded surface.} \label{fig:chse_v_chsse_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-019: We aim to answer these questions by analysing the dynamical properties of simple circuit models, composed of $2$-local quantum gates, and showing the consequences of the presence of scars, fragmentation and symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-020: It is accepted that in scarred and fragmented models certain initial states do not thermalise, and it immediately follows that such models cannot exhibit CHSE.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, the implications on the ``thermalising'' subspaces are not self-evident.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our main finding is that, in the presence of non-local conserved quantities, generic aperiodically driven systems can display CHSE within different subspaces, which we call Hilbert Subspace Ergodicity, schematically depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:chse_v_chsse_comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper is outlined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-024: In Section \ref{sec:background we provide a brief overview of the ETH and two ETH-violation mechanisms, QMBS and Hilbert space fragmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We also outline the notion of complete Hilbert space ergodicity.
elements.sent-2411-026: Section \ref{sec:chsse describes in detail the introduced notion of Complete Hilbert Subspace Ergodicity.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In Section \ref{sec:model we present the model used for all our numerical evidence, and in Sec.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We demonstrate the effects of scars in an otherwise CHSE system in Section \ref{sec:scars , and the effects of fragmentation in Section \ref{sec:fragmentation .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: For completeness, we also cover the effect of symmetries in Section \ref{sec:symmetries .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:conclusion we give a brief overview of results and their implications, as well as discuss potential avenues for future research.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Suppose that a state $\ket{\psi(0)}$ in a $D$-dimensional Hilbert space undergoes unitary dynamics, $\ket{\psi(t) = \hat{\mathbb{U (t)\ket{\psi(0)}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: For concreteness, we assume discrete-time dynamics where $\ket{\psi(t) = \hat{\mathbb{U _t\ket{\psi(t-1)}$ propagates the system forward one time-step from $t-1$ to $t$ and $\hat{\mathbb{U (t) = \hat{\mathbb{U _t \hat{\mathbb{U _{t-1 \hdots \hat{\mathbb{U _1$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: In analogy with the classical notion of ergodicity, CHSE means that given sufficient evolution time the quantum dynamics explores all of its available Hilbert space with equal likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-034: To be precise about the idea of ``equal likelihood'', one must introduce a measure on the Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-035: The natural choice is the unique measure ${\rm d}\phi$ that is invariant with respect to all unitary transformations, called the Haar measure.
elements.sent-2411-036: More formally then, CHSE means that \cite{Pil-23a : \begin{equation}\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T} f (\ket{\psi(t)}\bra{\psi(t)}) = \int {\rm d}\phi f (\ket{\phi}\bra{\phi}) , \label{eq:CHSE}\end{equation} for any initial state $\ket{\psi(0)}$ and for any integrable function $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In other words, if the system is CHSE then the long-time average of any observable quantity is indistinguishable from its Haar average.
elements.sent-2411-038: In practice one cannot test Eq.~\ref{eq:CHSE for all integrable functions $f$ and it is more convenient to restrict to polynomial functions of degree $k$, which can always be computed using a limited set of moments.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Following Ref.~\cite{Pil-23a , we define the $k$'th moment of the ensemble of states in the timeseries $\{ \ket{\psi(t) \}_{t=0 ^{T-1}$ as: \begin{equation}\hat{\rho}_T^{(k)} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} (\ket{\psi(t)}\bra{\psi(t)})^{\otimes k} , \label{eq:k_moment_time}\end{equation} and the $k$'th moment of the Haar ensemble of states as: \begin{equation}\hat{\rho}_{\rm Haar}^{(k)} = \int {\rm d}\phi (\ket{\phi}\bra{\phi})^{\otimes k}. \label{eq:k_moment_haar}\end{equation} We note that the $k$'th moment of the Haar ensemble can be computed exactly and has a closed form expression~\cite{Har-13a : \begin{equation}\label{HAS} \hat{\rho}^{(k)}_{\text{Haar}} = \frac{\sum_{\pi \in \mathcal{S}_k} \sum _{\alpha_1, ..., \alpha_k} | \alpha_1, \alpha_2, \hdots \alpha_k \rangle \langle \alpha_{\pi(1)}, \hdots \alpha_{\pi(k)}|}{D (D + 1) ... (D + k - 1)}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{S _k$ is the permutation group of order $k$ and $\{ \ket{\alpha_i \}_{i=1 ^D$ is an orthonormal basis for the Hilbert space of dimension $D$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: We denote the Hilbert-Schmidt distance between $\hat{\rho _T^{(k)}$ and $\hat{\rho ^{(k) _{\text{Haar }$ as: \begin{equation}\Delta_T^{(k)} = D_{HS}(\hat{\rho}^{(k)}_{\text{Haar}}, \hat{\rho}^{(k)}_T) = \| \hat{\rho}^{(k)}_{\text{Haar}} - \hat{\rho}^{(k)}_T \|_2^2, \label{eq:Delta_T^k}\end{equation} where $\| \hat{X \|_2 = \sqrt{{\rm Tr (\hat{X ^\dagger\hat{X )}$ is the Hilbert-Schmidt matrix norm \footnote{We note that Ref. \cite{Pil-23a used the trace distance instead of the Hilbert-Schmidt distance in Eq. \ref{eq:Delta_T^k .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The trace distance has the the advantage of a clear operational meaning in quantum state discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, to calculate it requires matrix diagonalisation while the Hilbert-Schmidt distance does not, and is easier to calculate numerically.. .
elements.sent-2411-043: Note that when originally introduced in Ref. \cite{Pil-23a , the CHSE was probed using the trace distance as a metric in Eq. \ref{eq:Delta_T^k .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, since the Hilbert-Schmidt distance can be used to upper bound the trace distance \cite{Col-19a and is significantly computationally cheaper to compute we will use the former.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Now, the system is CHSE if $\Delta_\infty^{(k) = 0$ for all $k$ and for all initial states $\ket{\psi(0)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Furthermore, in the case of a time-independent Hamiltonian, one can show that for $k \geq 2$ the trace distance to the Haar moments cannot be lower than \cite{Pil-23a : \begin{equation}B (D) \equiv (D + 1)^{-1} - (2D (D+1)) ^{-\frac{1}{2}},\end{equation} which one can adapt for the Hilbert-Schmidt distance.}

elements.sent-2411-048: Another quantity to probe CHSE is the ensemble entropy, recently introduced in Ref.~\cite{Mar-24 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: The key idea is to directly compare the states sampled by time evolution to states sampled from the Haar distribution.
elements.sent-2411-050: An ensemble is a collection of pure quantum states with associated probabilities: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ensemble} \mathcal{E} = \{p_j, \ket{\Psi _j} \},\end{equation} where $j$ can be a discrete value (corresponding to, for example, different measurement outcomes) or a continuous one (such as time).
elements.sent-2411-051: In the case of discretised probability distributions we will use index $j$ to refer to the different states, but in case of continuous variables we will use $\Psi$ itself as the variable and write the ensemble as: $\mathcal{E = \{P(\Psi), \ket{\Psi \}$ We will denote the Haar ensemble as $\mathcal{E _{Haar = \{P^H(\Psi), \ket{\Psi \}$, which will be the reference ensemble to compute the ensemble entropy.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: In the case of a discretised version of the Haar ensemble, we will use the notation $\mathcal{E _{Haar = \{p_j^H, \ket{\Psi _j \}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The ensemble generated by some aperiodic time evolution, also called the temporal ensemble, will be denoted as $\mathcal{E _T = \{P^T (\Psi), \ket{\Psi \}$ for the continuous version, and as $\mathcal{E _T = \{p_j^T, \ket{\Psi _j ^T \}$ for discretised one.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The ensemble entropy for this generated temporal ensemble, is then defined as the negative of the Kullback-Leibler divergence: \begin{equation}\label{eq: ens_ent} \text{Ent}(\mathcal{E}_T | \mathcal{E}_{\text{Haar}} ) = - \int d\Psi P^T(\Psi) {\log}_2 \left( \frac{P^T(\Psi)}{P^H (\Psi )}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: This quantity contains information about the Hilbert-Schmidt distances for different moments $k$: if we confirm that the ensemble entropy is close to $0$, that immediately implies that the moments will also be close to zero for any $k$.
elements.sent-2411-056: In practice, when numerically evaluating this measure we are using a finite set of states generated by the time evolution, $\{ \ket{\psi (t)}\}_{t = 0 ^{T-1}$, which demands a discretisation of Eq.~\eqref{eq: ens_ent , replacing the integral with a discrete summation over a finite set of states and associated probabilities, which we term as discretised ensemble entropy (DEE): \begin{equation}\label{eq: DEE} \text{DEE}(\mathcal{E}_T | \mathcal{E}_{\text{Haar}}) = - \sum_{j = 1}^M p_j^T \log _2 (\frac{p_j^T}{p^H_j}).\end{equation}.}}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Quantum Many-Body Scars and Hilbert Space Fragmentation
elements.sent-2411-058: One of the most important concepts to understand thermalisation of closed quantum systems is the eigenstate thermalisation hypothesis (ETH) \cite{Deu-91a, Sre-94a, Rig-08a, Deu-18a .}
elements.sent-2411-059: This phenomenon has been extensively studied and verified both numerically \cite{Bre-20a, Ste-13 and experimentally \cite{Tro-12, Clo-16, Kau-16, Tan-18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, in a seminal experiment performed on a 51-qubit array of Rydberg atoms, it was found that thermalisation fails when the system is initialised in specific initial states \cite{Ber-17 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The reason for the failure to thermalise is that the special initial states have a significant overlap with a small subset of ETH-violating eigenstates called quantum many body scars (QMBS) \cite{Tur-18a .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Since the first experiment, several more experiments have found evidence of QMBS in different systems \cite{Zha-22, Su-23 .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Also, a large amount of theoretical work has uncovered QMBS in various models, and layed the foundations for several scarring mechanisms \cite{Mou-22c .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Two of the most prominent approaches to encapsulate a wide range of scarred models are the projector-embedding \cite{Shi-17 and the Spectrum Generating Algebra (SGA) formalisms \cite{Mou-20a .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Another mechanism of ETH-violation is the so-called Hilbert space fragmentation (HSF), first explicitly identified in fracton models \cite{Pai-19, Sal-20, Khe-20 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In models with HSF, the Hilbert space ``shatters'' into exponentially many fragments, with respect to system size.
elements.sent-2411-067: Based on the scaling of the dimensions of these fragments, one can further distinguish between strong and weak fragmentation, which lead to strong and weak ETH-violation, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-068: Since weak HSF is very similar to QMBS in many aspects, it raised questions about the fundamental differences between these two phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-069: Ultimately, both of these mechanisms came to be understood within the framework of commutant algebras \cite{Mou-22a, Mou-22b .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{block_str.png} \caption{Schematic representation of the dynamically decoupled Krylov subspaces for chaotic systems (A), systems with QMBS (B), Hilbert space fragmentation (C), and conventional symmetries (D). A: In the case of chaotic systems, all states are thermal. B: For scarred systems, the majority of the states are thermal, while there is a vanishingly small number of QMBS states, with respect to system size. C: For systems with Hilbert space fragmentation, the number of decoupled subspaces scales exponentially with system size. The fundamental difference between systems with fragmentation and symmetries (D), is that in the former the number of decoupled subspaces is exponential in system size, while in the latter it is constant or at most polynomial. } \label{fig:subspace_decoupling}
elements.sent-2411-070: Common to both QMBS and HSF is the property that, starting from simple-to-prepare initial states, the dynamics can fail to lead to thermalisaion.
elements.sent-2411-071: This is because some simple initial states can be prepared that exist entirely in a disconnected, non-thermal subspace: in the case of models with QMBS, the scar states are effectively decoupled from the rest of the spectrum, and in models with HSF, there are exponentially many of these decoupled subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-072: This is schematically depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:subspace_decoupling .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Therefore both the Hamiltonian $\hat{H}$ and its corresponding unitary $\hat{U}$ will have a block diagonal structure.
elements.sent-2411-074: We will refer to these dynamically decoupled subspaces, represented by the different ``blocks'', as Krylov subspaces $\mathcal{K _\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: They are defined through the time evolution from different simple-to-prepare initial states $\ket{\psi_\alpha}$ (e.g., computational basis states) \cite{Mou-22a : \begin{equation}\mathcal{K}_{\alpha} \equiv \text{span}_t \{\hat{U}^{t} \ket{\psi _{\alpha}}\}\end{equation} Note that the time evolution of different initial states may generate the same subspace.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Therefore the full Hilbert space can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{H} = \bigoplus_{\alpha = 1}^{\mathcal{N}_{\mathcal{K}}} \mathcal{K}_{\alpha},\end{equation} where $\mathcal{N _{\mathcal{K }$ is the number of unique Krylov subspaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The number of distinct Krylov subspaces $\mathcal{N _{\mathcal{K }$ distinguishes between QMBS and HSF, while the size of the largest subspace, $D_{max}$, distinguishes between strong and weak fragmentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: If we have a constant, or polynomial number of Krylov subspaces, the system contains QMBS.
elements.sent-2411-079: If however the number of Krylov subspaces is exponential in system size, we can conclude the system exhibits HSF.
elements.sent-2411-080: Furthermore, if the dimension of the largest subspace, $D_{max}$, is a non-vanishing fraction of the total Hilbert space dimension, $\lim_{N \rightarrow \infty (D_{max /\text{dim (\mathcal{H ) )> 0$, it is an instance of weak fragmentation, whereas if this fraction is vanishing, $\lim_{N \rightarrow \infty (D_{max /\text{dim (\mathcal{H )) = 0$, it is an example of strong fragmentation \cite{Sal-20 .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Signatures of strong and weak violation of the ETH can also be observed in the growth of the bipartition entanglement entropy and the decay of the infinite temperature autocorrelator, which are discussed in more detail in the Appendices \ref{app:bipartite_entanglement_entropy , \ref{app:autoccorelators .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The original notion of CHSE was introduced for generic aperiodic drives without any conserved quantities.
elements.sent-2411-083: However, in the presence of (local or non-local) conserved quantities the Hilbert space may decouple into several Krylov subspaces $\mathcal{K _{\alpha}$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Because of this decoupling, it will be impossible to exhibit CHSE in the full Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-085: However, the system may still ``explore'' the subspaces with equal probability, which we term Complete Hilbert Subspace Ergodicity (CHSSE).

elements.sent-2411-094: The chain of qudits evolves dynamically by a ``brickwork'' quantum circuit, made up of layers of two-qubit nearest-neighbour unitaries.
elements.sent-2411-095: We choose our brickwork circuit so that each timestep is completely specified by the two ``bricks'' $\hat{U _e = \exp (i\hat{H _e)$ and $\hat{U _o = \exp (i\hat{H _o)$, where $\hat{U _e$ makes up the even layer $\hat{\mathbb{U _e = \hat{U _e \otimes \hat{U _e \otimes \hdots $ of the brickwork, and $\hat{U _o$ makes up the odd layer $\hat{\mathbb{U _o = \hat{\mathbb{I \otimes \hat{U _o \otimes \hat{U _o \otimes \hdots$ (see Fig. \ref{fig:brickwork for an illustration).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-097: However, to generate CHSE, our circuit cannot be periodic in time.
elements.sent-2411-098: So, we introduce two different unitary propagators: $\hat{\mathbb{U ^{(A)}$ [specified by the local unitary bricks $\hat{U _e^{(A)}$, $\hat{U _o^{(A)}$] and $\hat{\mathbb{U ^{(B)}$ [specified by the bricks $\hat{U _e^{(B)}$, $\hat{U _o^{(B)}$].}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-100: We set two initial words $W_0 = 1$, $W_1 = 0$, and then construct longer words using the rule $W_{j + 1 = W_j W _{j - 1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: This rule can be repeated infinitely many times to give $W_{\infty}$.

elements.sent-2411-172: A known model that displays strong Hilbert space fragmentation for local Hilbert space dimension $d \geq 3$ is the so-called pair-flip model \cite{Cah-18a , which was originally studied as a Hamiltonian model, but can also be implemented as a gate model.}

elements.sent-2411-174: Effectively, this model has the constraint that two neighbouring sites can change their state if and only if they are in the same state, as seen in the first term.

elements.sent-2411-211: Complete Hilbert Space Ergodicity has extended the notion of ergodicity from classical to certain quantum systems undergoing aperiodic dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-212: It can thus be seen as complementary to the ETH, which has been conceptually crucial for our understanding of thermalisation in Hamiltonian and Floquet systems.
elements.sent-2411-213: Through many experiments and numerical demonstrations, different ETH violating mechanisms have been identified, including QMBS and HSF.
elements.sent-2411-214: In this work, we presented the effects of these ergodicity breaking mechanisms on systems which otherwise display CHSE.
elements.sent-2411-215: In both scarred and fragmented models, the presence of non-local conserved quantities, leads to a decoupling between different subspaces of the full Hilbert space.

elements.sent-2411-323: Ergodicity has been one of the fundamental concepts underpinning our understanding of thermalization in isolated systems since the first developments in classical statistical mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-324: Recently, a similar notion has been introduced for quantum systems, termed complete Hilbert space ergodicity (CHSE), in which the evolving quantum state explores all of the available Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-325: This contrasts with the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH), in which thermalization is formulated via the properties of matrix elements of local operators in the energy eigenbasis.
elements.sent-2411-326: In this work we explore how ETH-violation mechanisms, including quantum many-body scars and Hilbert space fragmentation can affect complete Hilbert space ergodicity.
elements.sent-2411-327: We find that the presence of these mechanisms leads to CHSE in decoupled subspaces, a phenomenon we call Hilbert subspace ergodicity, and which represents a protocol for constructing $t$-designs in subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-328: This has spurred significant research interest into how thermalization can be understood in isolated quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-329: The most widely accepted framework in this area is the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH) \cite{Deu-91a, Sre-94a, Rig-08a, Deu-18a .}
elements.sent-2411-330: The ETH states that the apparent thermalization of quantum systems occurs due to the fact that eigenstates of large, generic many body systems appear indistinguishable from thermal states at the level of local observables.
elements.sent-2411-331: In contrast to ETH, recently Pilatewski et al. \cite{Pil-23a introduced the concept of complete Hilbert space ergodicity (CHSE).}
elements.sent-2411-332: What is the effect of ETH-violating mechanisms on the dynamics of the systems which would otherwise exhibit CHSE? What is the effect of symmetries? Can the notion of CHSE be generalized to these cases, where the many-body systems contain conserved quantities?.
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{schematic_comparison.png} \caption{Schematic representation for complete Hilbert space ergodicity (A) vs. complete Hilbert space ergodicity in a subspace (B). In the former, the system explores all of the available $D$-dimensional Hilbert space, represented by a three-dimensional box. In the latter however, the system can only explore a $B$-dimensional subspace of the full Hilbert space, represented by a two-dimensional horizontal slice. In the long time limit, the system represented in panel (A) will explore all regions within the box with equal probability, whereas the system in panel (B) will explore just the shaded surface.} \label{fig:chse_v_chsse_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-333: We aim to answer these questions by analyzing the dynamical properties of simple circuit models, composed of $2$-local quantum gates, and showing the consequences of the presence of scars, fragmentation and symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-334: However, the implications on the ``thermalizing'' subspaces are not self-evident.
elements.sent-2411-335: Our main finding is that, in the presence of nonlocal conserved quantities, generic aperiodically driven systems can display CHSE within different subspaces, which we call Hilbert Subspace Ergodicity, schematically depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:chse_v_chsse_comparison .}

elements.sent-2411-500: In the case of an ergodic system with scars, and starting in a nonscar initial state $\ket{\psi _0}$, we expect the bipartition entanglement entropy to approach the Page value \cite{Pag-93a .}
elements.sent-2411-501: Starting in a scar state leads to no entanglement growth, whereas the entanglement entropy for the chosen nonscar state quickly grows and saturates around the Page value.
elements.sent-2411-502: If the system is initialized in one of the frozen states, then it remains in the frozen state for all times, and therefore the entanglement entropy stays at $0$.
elements.sent-2411-503: However, if the system is initialized in any state in the largest subspace, then it will quickly increase to the Page value and oscillate around it.
elements.figure-2411-032: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{bw_ent_evo.png} \caption{Growth of bipartition entanglement entropy for the generic brickwork model, starting from two initial states: $\ket{\psi (0)} = \ket{0}^{\otimes N}$ and $\ket{\psi (0)} = \ket{+}^{\otimes N}$, for $N = 6, \; d= 2$. The results are averaged over 100 different instances, with the lines corresponding to the average distances, and the shaded areas represent the 10th-90th percentile range. Both initial states eventually saturate around $S_{\text{Page}} = \frac{1}{2}N \text{ln}(d) - \frac{1}{2}$.} \label{fig:bw_entanglement_entropy}
elements.figure-2411-033: \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{scars_ent_entropy_evo.png} \caption{Time evolution of bipartite entanglement entropy for the initial scar state $\ket{S_1}$ and nonscar state $\ket{1}^{\otimes N}$, for QMBS model with $N = 6, \; d =2$. For the scar state (violet) the entanglement entropy stays at 0, which is expected for a product state. But for the nonscar state (red), the entanglement entropy instantaneously grows to the Page entropy, $S_{\text{Page}} = \frac{1}{2}N \text{ln}(d) - \frac{1}{2}$, which is the expected entanglement entropy for a Haar random state. Note that the line represents the average over the 100 iterations and the shaded region represents the 10th-90th percentiles.} \label{fig:single_qmbs_entropy}
elements.figure-2411-034: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{hsf_ent_evo.png} \caption{Growth of bipartite entanglement entropy for a set of chosen initial states, using the HSF model with $N = 6, \; d = 3$. When starting in states belonging to the largest, $15$-dimensional subspace (blue, purple, brown), the entanglement entropy quickly achieves, and oscillates around the Page value (dashed black line). The states belonging to $7$-dimensional subspaces (red, green, orange), oscillate close around half of the Page value.} \label{fig:hsf_ee_growth}
elements.figure-2411-035: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{autocorrelator_v2.png} \caption{Comparison of the decay of the infinite temperature autocorrelator $\mathcal{A}$, as defined in Eq.~\eqref{eq:autocorrelator}, for the generic brickwork model (GBW), scarred model (QMBS), and the model with Hilbert space fragmentation (HSF). For all of these we use system size $N = 6$. The local Hilbert space dimension is $d = 2$ for GBW and QMBS models, while we use $d = 3$ for the HSF one. The local observable used to compute the autocorrelator for QMBS and GBW is $\hat{\sigma}^{z}_{N/2}$, and the one for HSF is $\hat{S}^{z}_{N/2}$, where $\hat{S}^z = \ket{1}\bra{1} - \ket{-1}\bra{-1}$. We observe that in the GBW the autocorrelator decays to almost exactly $0$, while for the QMBS it decays almost to $0$. Note that this small deviation is exponentially small in systems size and would vanish in the thermodynamic limit. In the case of HSF however, the autocorrelation function saturates to a finite bound of $1/3$, which is independent of system size, and indicates strong ergodicity breaking.} \label{fig:autocorrelator_comparison}
